1: A shadow: 'I have come to my garden, my sister, my bride; I gathered my myrrh with my spice.' The groom enters the garden — the response to the bride's invitation in Song of Solomon 4:16. The incarnation is this entering: the Word became flesh and dwelt among us (John 1:14). Christ comes into the human garden not as a visitor but as one who gathers and eats and drinks — the full participation in creaturely life. 'Eat, O friends, and drink: drink deeply, O beloved ones!' is the invitation to share in the abundance of the divine-human communion.
2: A shadow: 'I slept, but my heart was awake. A voice — my beloved knocking: Open to me.' The parable of the ten virgins (Matthew 25:1-13) uses the same structure: some are ready when the bridegroom comes, some are not. Here the bride is half-asleep when the knock comes — not the failure of the foolish virgins but the spiritual drowsiness that Christ warns against (Mark 13:35-37: stay awake; you do not know when the master of the house will come). Christ's knock and voice at the door (Revelation 3:20) is the same appeal as this midnight knocking: 'Open to me.'
3: A revelation of God: 'I have taken off my robe — how can I put it back on? I have washed my feet — how can I dirty them again?' The bride's hesitation — the cost of responding. She is comfortable; the Beloved's call requires getting up, getting dressed, dirtying the feet. Jesus's teaching on the cost of discipleship (Luke 9:23: 'take up your cross daily and follow me'; Luke 14:33: 'whoever of you does not renounce all that he has cannot be my disciple') is the same dynamic: the call of Christ requires inconvenience, disruption, the willingness to dirty the feet again for his sake.
4: A revelation of God: 'My beloved thrust his hand through the latch opening, and I felt a deep longing for him.' The persistence of Christ who comes even when the door is not immediately opened — 'I stand at the door and knock; if anyone hears my voice and opens the door, I will come in' (Revelation 3:20). The bride's 'deep longing' is stirred by the very hesitation and delay: sometimes what awakens desire most forcefully is the moment when it was almost refused. Grace can work through the experience of near-loss.
5: A shadow: 'My hands dripped with myrrh — my fingers with flowing myrrh — on the handles of the bolt.' The myrrh on the handles is the fragrance of the Beloved's own hand — he has left his presence on the very door she finally opens. The myrrh of Christ's passion (the burial spice, John 19:39) marks the point of entry: the way into the Beloved's presence runs through the cross. The door of grace is the door marked with the sacrifice of Christ, just as the Passover doorposts were marked with blood (Exodus 12:7).
6: A shadow: 'I opened to my beloved, but he had gone. I was beside myself when he left.' The opened door and the absent Beloved — the experience of seeking the risen Christ and finding the empty tomb. Mary Magdalene's grief at the empty tomb (John 20:11-13: they have taken away my Lord, and I do not know where they have laid him) is the direct NT parallel. The Beloved's withdrawal is not rejection but is a pattern of the divine-human relationship: moments of absence drive the seeking heart to find Christ in a new way.
7: A revelation of God: The watchmen who beat and wounded the seeking bride — the suffering of the church as she seeks Christ in a hostile world. The NT pattern is the Passion and Acts: the disciples scattered when the Shepherd was struck (Zechariah 13:7; Matthew 26:31); Stephen, Paul, and the apostles bear wounds from those who guard the city's order while the church seeks the risen Lord. The stripping of the mantle (veil, honor) parallels the shaming of Christ's followers for their loyalty to him (Hebrews 13:13: let us go to him outside the camp, bearing his reproach).
8: A revelation of God: 'If you find my beloved, tell him I am faint with love.' The second use of this phrase (2:5 was 'I am faint with love' from the excess of his presence; 5:8 is the same faintness from his absence) — the same overwhelming love in both conditions. Paul's analogous statement: 'I am hard pressed between the two. My desire is to depart and be with Christ, for that is far better' (Philippians 1:23). The faintness of love is not weakness but the sign that the relationship with Christ is the deepest center of the soul's life.
9: A revelation of God: The daughters of Jerusalem ask what makes the bride's beloved superior — 'what makes him so special that you beg this of us?' The question invites the wasf of Song of Solomon 5:10-16 — the church's description of Christ to the world. This is the dynamic of evangelism: the world asks what makes Christ different from any other beloved, and the church answers with a description of his person and beauty. 'Always be prepared to give a reason for the hope that is in you' (1 Peter 3:15). The description begins with Song of Solomon 5:10.
10: A revelation of God: 'My beloved is radiant and rugged, outstanding among ten thousand men.' The description of the Beloved begins at the top — radiant (dazzling white), rugged (ruddy, healthy strength). The NT's vision of the risen Christ (Revelation 1:13-16: his face like the sun shining in full strength; his eyes like flames of fire) is the unveiled form of the beauty the bride describes. 'Outstanding among ten thousand' is the incomparability of Christ: there is no other mediator, no other Savior, no other name (Acts 4:12).
11: A revelation of God: 'His head is like purest gold; his hair is wavy and dark as a raven.' The gold head recalls the vision in Daniel 7:9 (the Ancient of Days whose hair was white as wool) and Revelation 1:14 (the Son of Man whose head and hair were white as white wool). The divine majesty expressed through the imagery of precious metal and perfect appearance: the one the bride describes is no ordinary beloved. The raven-dark hair in the earthly wasf becomes white as wool in the eschatological vision — the same person seen in two frames of time.
12: A revelation of God: 'His eyes are like doves beside streams of water — bathed in milk, perfectly set.' The dove-eyes of Song of Solomon 1:15 (where the bride was praised with this image) are now attributed to the Beloved — the mutuality of the love is mirrored in the imagery. The eyes of Christ are his attention to his people: 'the eyes of the LORD are toward the righteous and his ears toward their cry' (Psalms 34:15). The eyes 'bathed in milk' convey purity and clarity of vision — the all-seeing gaze that is not surveillance but pure love.
13: A revelation of God: 'His cheeks are like beds of spice, banks of sweet herbs. His lips are lilies dripping with flowing myrrh.' The beloved's lips are lilies dripping myrrh — beauty and sacrifice together. The words of Christ are 'grace and truth' (John 1:17) — the grace of the lily's beauty, the truth of the myrrh's honesty about suffering. 'No one ever spoke like this man' (John 7:46). The lips of the beloved produce words that are simultaneously fragrant (grace) and sacrificial (myrrh).
14: A shadow: Gold cylinders set with jewels, carved ivory overlaid with sapphires — the materials of the Tabernacle and Temple: gold, precious stones, ivory. The beloved's body described in Tabernacle terms: he is the dwelling-place of God in visible form. The NT's direct statement: 'in him all the fullness of the Deity dwells bodily' (Colossians 2:9). The beloved described as a walking sanctuary is the shadow the incarnation brings to its fulfillment.
15: A shadow: 'His legs are like columns of alabaster, set in sockets of gold. His form is like Lebanon — magnificent as the cedars.' The alabaster columns set in gold evoke the temple columns (1 Kings 7:15-22: the two bronze pillars Jachin and Boaz set at the entrance to the temple). The Beloved's form is the form of the holy place. John 2:19-21 is the interpretive key: the temple is his body. What the Solomonic temple embodied architecturally, Christ embodied personally — the dwelling of God with humanity.
16: A revelation of God: 'His mouth is sweetness itself; he is altogether desirable. This is my beloved and this is my friend, O daughters of Jerusalem.' The climax of the wasf: mouth = sweetness, whole person = altogether desirable, and the double identification: my beloved AND my friend. The NT expresses the same double identity: 'I no longer call you servants ... I have called you friends' (John 15:15). Christ is not only the majestic Lord whose description fills Song of Solomon 5:10-16 but also the intimate Friend whose knowledge of us is complete and whose love is personal.
